title: Ontology-Oriented Software Development
summary: Peter Wilczynski (Product Lead for Ontology System) argues that enterprise software fails because it optimizes components in isolation rather than at the system level. The Palantir Ontology centralizes knowledge about data, logic, and action elements into a shared system, enabling ontology-oriented development via the OSDK. This allows programmers to write code in the language of the business (Airplanes, Flight Schedules, Airports) rather than in terms of component-specific APIs. The Ontology exposes three interface types: GUI, API, and Natural Language Interfaces.
relevantParts: Defines the Ontology as integrating data, logic, and action elements into a shared decision-centric model
Introduces OSDK (Ontology SDK) as the developer interface to the Ontology in TypeScript, Python, and Java
Shows concrete code comparison: component-centric (fragmented API calls) vs ontology-oriented (OSDK with typed business objects)
Explains three interface types: GUI (visual), API (programmatic/AI), NLI (natural language)
Positions AI systems as interacting with APIs like applications, making the Ontology essential for enterprise AI

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Shared decision-centric model integrating data, logic, and action elements from across the enterprise IT landscape

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: Ontology Software Development Kit - exposes Ontology data, logic, and action elements in idiomatic Python, TypeScript, and Java

extractedEntities.name: Data Elements
extractedEntities.description: Representations of enterprise data integrated from core transactional systems, SaaS tools, and internal systems

extractedEntities.name: Logic Elements
extractedEntities.description: Computational components including predictive models, optimization algorithms, and business rules

extractedEntities.name: Action Elements
extractedEntities.description: Operational components that write back decisions to appropriate systems and distribute notifications

extractedEntities.name: GUI
extractedEntities.description: Graphical User Interfaces providing visual representations of the Ontology

extractedEntities.name: API
extractedEntities.description: Application Programming Interfaces providing programmers and AI systems direct access to the Ontology

extractedEntities.name: NLI
extractedEntities.description: Natural Language Interfaces allowing humans and language-based AI systems to interact with the Ontology

extractedEntities.name: AIP
extractedEntities.description: AI Platform providing foundational capabilities and development toolchain for building bespoke AI systems on the Ontology

keyInsights: The Ontology is analogous to a 'somewhat sophisticated monorepo which enforces certain conventions leading to a more composable system'
Ontology knowledge compounds: new applications leverage preexisting system integration work
Low-code builders can leverage code-based data, logic, and action elements via the Ontology
The OSDK allows seamless migration from low-code to pro-code without reimplementation
AI systems interact directly with APIs (like applications), making the Ontology essential for enterprise AI
The Ontology represents a single language expressible in graphical, verbal, and programmatic forms

title: How Palantir AIP Reduces Hallucinations
summary: Explains three techniques for reducing LLM hallucinations using the Palantir Ontology in AIP: (1) grounding LLMs in trusted Ontology data via query tools, (2) delegating computation to specialized logic tools/functions instead of relying on LLM token prediction, and (3) implementing human review of AI-suggested actions through approval workflows. Demonstrates Ontology Augmented Generation (OAG) as a key pattern.
relevantParts: Three concrete techniques: data grounding, logic tool delegation, human review of actions
Introduces Ontology Augmented Generation (OAG) as alternative to standard RAG
Shows AIP Logic functions with tool-equipped LLMs querying the Ontology
Demonstrates how Object Types and Properties ground LLM responses in trusted data
Human-AI teaming pattern: queue AI-suggested actions for domain expert approval

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Augmented Generation (OAG)
extractedEntities.description: Augmenting LLM prompts with Ontology metadata to guide generation of search queries executed against trusted Ontology data

extractedEntities.name: Logic Tool Delegation
extractedEntities.description: LLM orchestrates handoffs to specialized models, functions, or rules for tasks requiring computation rather than text generation

extractedEntities.name: Human-AI Teaming
extractedEntities.description: AI-generated actions are queued for human approval rather than executed directly, providing oversight layer

extractedEntities.name: AIP Logic
extractedEntities.description: Development environment for building LLM-powered workflows with tool-equipped functions that can query the Ontology

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Tools
extractedEntities.description: Query tools and custom Functions provided to LLMs within AIP Logic to ground responses in trusted data and logic

keyInsights: LLMs are designed for next-token prediction, not truthful question answering — hallucinations are inherent to their generative nature
Grounding LLMs in Ontology data works by augmenting prompts with Ontology metadata (object types and properties) so the LLM generates search queries executed against trusted data
Logic tools (custom Functions in the Ontology) handle computation tasks LLMs are unsuited for (distance calculations, forecasting, simulations)
Three-layer defense: trusted data + trusted logic + human review
The Ontology's decision-centric model (data + logic + action) maps naturally to the three hallucination reduction strategies

title: Purpose-based Access Controls at Palantir
summary: Describes Palantir Foundry's Purpose-Based Access Control system where users apply for access to a 'Purpose' (a scoped collection of data) rather than individual datasets. Data governance teams set Purposes, must record rationales for access decisions, and non-technical administrators can enforce controls directly. This enables auditing of not just who has access, but why they were given access.
relevantParts: Purpose-Based Access Controls as a unique Foundry security primitive
Users apply for access to Purposes, not individual datasets — data is scoped to the Purpose
Mandatory rationale recording for access decisions — enables auditing of 'why' not just 'who'
Non-technical governance administrators can enforce controls without relying on technical colleagues
Partitioning data processing into Purposes with assigned owners prevents governance blind spots

extractedEntities.name: Purpose-Based Access Controls (PBAC)
extractedEntities.description: Access control model where users apply for access to a Purpose (scoped data collection) rather than individual datasets

extractedEntities.name: Purpose
extractedEntities.description: A governance-defined scope containing data specifically selected to help users meet their goal — no more, no less

extractedEntities.name: Rationale Recording
extractedEntities.description: Mandatory justification capture when granting access or approving data use for a Purpose

extractedEntities.name: Purpose Owner
extractedEntities.description: Assigned accountability role for a specific Purpose, preventing governance blind spots

keyInsights: Users access Purposes, not individual datasets — data governance teams scope data to each Purpose
Both data owners and data governance teams must record rationales, creating a persistent audit trail
Non-technical administrators can enforce access controls directly without technical intermediaries
Partitioning into Purposes with owners prevents formation of unmonitored 'dark areas'
Model aligns with GDPR purpose limitation principle
